title: Ingrown Dog Toenails: What to Do When a Nail Grows Into the Paw Pad
meta_desc: Is your dog's nail growing into their paw pad? Learn about ingrown nails, urgency levels, and why professional veterinary care is essential.
content_html:
What Is an Ingrown Toenail?

An ingrown or embedded toenail occurs when a dog's claw grows long enough to curl back and pierce the sensitive skin of the paw pad. This condition is most frequently seen in the dewclaws—the 'thumb' nails located higher up on the leg—because they do not naturally wear down against the ground. When the nail breaks the skin, it creates an open wound that is highly susceptible to bacteria and debris.

Is This an Emergency?

This situation is classified as a medium urgency level. While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is a very painful condition that requires professional veterinary intervention within 24 to 48 hours. You should not attempt to pull or cut the embedded nail at home, as the nail may be hooked deep into the tissue, and the pain may cause even the gentlest dog to snap or bite out of reflex.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photo of the affected paw to a triage professional is incredibly helpful. It allows the veterinary team to assess the depth of the nail's penetration and look for signs of a secondary infection, such as significant swelling, discharge, or 'angry' red tissue. This visual information helps determine if your dog needs immediate urgent care or if the appointment can wait until the following morning.

faqs.question: Can I just cut the nail myself if my dog lets me?
faqs.answer: It is strongly discouraged. Because the nail is embedded in the flesh, cutting it can cause sudden, intense pain and significant bleeding. A veterinarian can provide local numbing or sedation to remove the nail safely and painlessly.

faqs.question: What are the signs that the paw pad is infected?
faqs.answer: Common signs of infection include a foul odor, yellow or green discharge (pus), extreme heat in the paw, and the dog obsessively licking the area or refusing to put weight on the leg.

faqs.question: How can I prevent this from happening again?
faqs.answer: The best prevention is regular nail trims every 4 to 6 weeks. Pay special attention to the dewclaws, as they do not touch the ground and are the most likely to curl into the skin.
